en.content: A beam of light travelling in water strikes a glass plate which is also immersed in water. When the angle of incidence is $60^{\circ}$, the reflected beam is found to be plane polarised. Find the refractive index of glass, if refractive index of water is $\sqrt{2}$.
en.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { According to Brewster's law }\\ &\begin{aligned} & & \mu_{g o} & =\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_w}=\tan i_p=\sqrt{3} \\ \therefore & & \mu_g & =\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{2}=\sqrt{6} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: Consider a hydrogen atom with its electron in the $n$th orbit. An electromagnetic radiation of wavelength 90 nm is used to ionize the atom. If the kinetic energy of the ejected electron is 10.4 eV , then the value of $n$ is ( $h c=1242 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm}$ )
en.explanation:
Kinetic energy of ejected electron $=$ Energy of incident photon - Energy required to ionize the electron from $n$th orbit (all in eV )

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 10.4=\frac{1242}{90}-\left|E_n\right| \\ & 10.4=13.8-\frac{13.6}{n^2} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{13.6}{n^2}=3.4 \\ & \left(\text { as } E_n \propto \frac{1}{n^2} \text { and } E_1=-13.6 \mathrm{eV}\right) \end{array} $$

Solving this equation, we get

               $$ n=2 $$

en.content: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area $A$ and separation between plates $d$ is charged with constant current $I$. Consider a plane surface of area $\frac{5 A}{6}$ parallel to the plates and drawn between the plates. The displacement current through area is
en.explanation:
The charge on the capacitor plates at time $t$ is given by $q=I t$

Electric field $=\varepsilon=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{q}{A \varepsilon_0}=\frac{I t}{A \varepsilon_0}$

Flux through area $\frac{5 \mathrm{~A}}{6}$ is $\phi_{\varepsilon}=\left(\frac{5 \mathrm{~A}}{6}\right) \varepsilon$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{\varepsilon}=\frac{5 \mathrm{~A}}{6} \times \frac{I t}{A \varepsilon_0} \Rightarrow \phi_{\varepsilon}=\frac{5 I t}{6 \varepsilon_0} $$

Now, displacement current $=I_d=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi_e}{d t}$

                                         $$ \begin{aligned} & I_d=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{5 I t}{6 \varepsilon_0}\right] \\ & I_d=\frac{5}{6} I \end{aligned} $$

en.content: A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of Earth with a velocity equal to one third of escape velocity. The maximum height attained by the body will be
en.explanation:
Escape velocity, $V_e=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}$

From conservation of energy

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{KE}_i+\mathrm{PE}_i & =\mathrm{KE}_f+\mathrm{PE}_f \\ \frac{-G M m}{R}+\frac{1 m}{2} \frac{V_e^2}{9} & =\frac{-G M m}{R+h} \\ \frac{G M}{R+h} & =\frac{G M}{R}-\frac{V_e^2}{18} \quad\left(\therefore V=\frac{V_e}{3}\right) \\ \frac{G M}{R+h} & =\frac{G M}{R}-\frac{G M}{9 R} \quad\left(\therefore V_e=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}\right) \\ \frac{G M}{R+h} & =\frac{8 G M}{9 R} \\ \frac{1}{R+h} & =\frac{8}{9 R} \end{aligned} $$

                                    $$ \begin{aligned} & R=8 h \Rightarrow h=\frac{R}{8} \\ & h=\frac{6400}{8}=800 \mathrm{~km} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: A string of length $L$ and force constant $k$ is stretched to obtain extension $l$. It is further stretched to obtain extension $l_1$. The work done in second stretching is
en.explanation:
Work done in stretching a string to obtain an extension l,

$$ W_1=\frac{1}{2} k l^2 $$

Similarly, work done in stretching a string to obtain an extension $l_1$ is,

$$ W_2=\frac{1}{2} k l_1^2 $$

∴ Work done in second case, $W$

$$ =W_2-W_1=\frac{1}{2} k\left(l_1^2-l^2\right) $$

en.content: In a Young's double slit experiment for a particular wavelength of light the distance between third dark and fifth bright fringe is 1.63 mm . If the distance between two slit is 1 mm and distance between slit and screen is 1 metre. Then, the wavelength of light is
en.explanation:
For 5th bright fringe

$$ x^5=n \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{5 \times 1 \times \lambda}{1 \times 10^{-3}} $$

and 3rd dark fringe

$$ x^3=\frac{(2 n-1) D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{5 \times 1 \times \lambda}{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}} $$

Now, $x^5-x^3=1.63 \mathrm{~mm}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{5 \lambda}{10^{-3}}-\frac{5 \lambda}{2 \times 10^{-3}} & =1.63 \times 10^{-3} \\ \frac{5 \lambda}{2} & =1.63 \times 10^{-3} \\ \lambda & =\frac{3.26}{5} \times 10^{-6}=6.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: A mass $M$ is broken into two parts of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$. How are $m_1$ and $m_2$ related, so that force of gravitational attraction between the two parts is maximum?
en.explanation:
Let $m_1=m$, then $m_2=M-m$

Then, $F=\frac{G m_1 m_2}{r^2}=\frac{G m(M-m)}{r^2}$

$$ =\frac{G}{r^2}\left(M m-m^2\right) $$

Differentiating w.r.t. m , we get

$$ \frac{d F}{d m}=\frac{G}{r^2}(M-2 m) $$

For $F$ to be maximum,

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{d F}{d m} & =0 \\ \frac{G}{r^2}(M-2 m) & =0 \\ M-2 m & =0 \\ 2 m & =M \\ m & =\frac{M}{2} \end{aligned} $$

So, $m_1=\frac{M}{2}$

$$ m_2=M-\frac{M}{2}=\frac{M}{2} $$

en.content: The frequency of a light wave in a material is $2 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ and wavelength is $5000 \mathop {\rm{A}}\limits^{\rm{o}} $. The refractive index of material will be
en.explanation:
Velocity of light waves in material is

$$ v=\nu \lambda \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Refractive index of material is

$$ \mu=\frac{c}{v} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$$

where, $c$ is speed of light in vacuum or air.

or   $$ \mu=\frac{c}{v \lambda} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { }\\ &\begin{aligned} Given,\,\, \nu & =2 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz} \\ \lambda & =5000 \mathop {\rm{A}}\limits^{\rm{o}} =5000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \\ c & =3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \\ \mu & =\frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^{14} \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}=3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: A man with a mass of 80 kg is standing on the rim to a circular platform with a mass of 200 kg . The circular platform is rotating at 12 revolutions per minute (rpm) about its axis. The man moves from the rim towards the centre of the platform. The new angular velocity of the system will be (Assuming that the man's moment of inertia at the centre of the plateform is negligible)
en.explanation:
According to the law of conservation of angular momentum.

Total initial angular momentum = Total final angular momentum

$$ \begin{aligned} \left(I+m R^2\right) \omega & =I \omega^{\prime} \\ \therefore \quad \omega^{\prime} & =\frac{I+m R^2}{I} \omega \Rightarrow \omega^{\prime}=\frac{\frac{M R^2}{2}+m R^2}{\frac{M R^2}{2}} \omega \\ \text { or } \quad \omega^{\prime} & =\frac{M+2 m}{M}=\frac{200+2 \times 80}{200} \times 12 \\ \omega^{\prime} & =\frac{360}{200} \times 12=21.6 \mathrm{rpm} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: A pure resistive circuit element $X$, when connected to an AC supply of peak voltage 200 V , gives a peak current of 5 A . A second circuit element $Y$, when connected to the same AC supply also gives the same value of peak current but the current lags behind by $90^{\circ}$. If the series combination of $X$ and $Y$ is connected to the same supply, what will be the rms value of current?
en.explanation:
Clearly first element is resistance and second element is inductor

$$ \begin{aligned} R & =X_L=\frac{200}{5}=40 \Omega \\ \therefore Z & =\sqrt{R_f^2+X_L^2}=40 \sqrt{2} \Omega \\ I_{\mathrm{rms}} & =\frac{V_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}=\frac{200 / \sqrt{2}}{40 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe is same as the first overtone frequency of an open organ pipe. If the length of open organ pipe is 50 cm , then the length of closed organ pipe is
en.explanation:
For closed organ pipe, fundamental frequency,

$$ f_1=\frac{v}{4 l_c} $$

For open organ pipe, first overtone frequency,

$$ f_2=\frac{2 v}{2 l_0}=\frac{v}{l_0} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Now, } f_1=f_2 \Rightarrow \frac{v}{4 l_c}=\frac{v}{l_0}\\ &\therefore l_c=\frac{l_0}{4}=\frac{50}{4}=12.5 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: A vessel is filled with a gas at a pressure of 76 cm of mercury at a certain temperature. The mass of gas is increased by $50 \%$ introducing more gas in the vessel at the same temperature. Now the resultant pressure of the gas is
en.explanation:
According to kinetic theory of gases

$$ p V=\frac{1}{3} M V_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 $$

at constant temperature $V_{\text {rms }}^2$ is constant, as $V$ is also constant, so $p \propto M$

When the mass of gas increases by $50 \%$ pressure also increases by 50\%.

So, final pressure $=76+\frac{50}{100} \times 76=114 \mathrm{~cm}$ of Hg .

en.content: The mass of proton is 1.0073 u and that of neutron is $1.0087 \mathrm{u}(\mathrm{u}=$ atomic mass unit). The binding energy of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4$ is
en.explanation:
The nucleus of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4$ contains 2 neutrons and 2 protons.

Mass of 2 protons $=2 \times 1.0073=2.0146 u$

Mass of 2 neutrons $=2 \times 1.0087=2.0174 u$

Total mass of 2 protons and 2 neutrons

$$ =(2.0146+2.0174)=4.032 u $$

Mass of helium nucleus $=4.0015 u$

Thus, mass defect is lacking of mass in forming the helium nucleus from 2 protons and 2 neutrons.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \Delta m & =\text { Mass defect } \\ & =(4.032-4.0015) \mathrm{u}=0.0305 \mathrm{u} \\ & =0.0305 \mathrm{amu} \end{aligned} $$

but $1 \mathrm{amu}=931 \mathrm{MeV}$

Hence, binding energy,

$$ \begin{aligned} \Delta E & =(\Delta m) \times 931 \\ & =0.0305 \times 931=28.4 \mathrm{MeV} \end{aligned} $$

$$ D=2 x=2 \times 2.5=5 \mathrm{~cm} $$

en.content: A bar magnet has magnetic moment of $0.05 \mathrm{Am}^2$ which is suspended in uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T . Calculate the work done in rotating the magnet from its most stable to most unstable position in the magnetic field.
en.explanation:
For most stable state, $\theta_1=0^{\circ}$ and for most unstable state $\theta_2=180^{\circ}$

∴ Work done $=U_{\text {final }}-U_{\text {initial }}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & W=-m B \cos \left(180^{\circ}\right)-\left(-m B \cos 0^{\circ}\right) \\ & W=-m B(-1)+m B=2 m B \\ & W=2 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \\ & W=0.20 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: A body is projected with a speed $u \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle $\beta$ with the horizontal. The kinetic energy at the highest point is $(3 / 4)$ th of the initial kinetic energy. The value of $\beta$ is
en.explanation:
As the vertical component of the velocity at highest point is zero.

$$ u_y=0, u_x=u \cos \beta $$

The kinetic energy at the highest point will be equal to $\frac{1}{2} m(u \cos \beta)^2$

The initial kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2} m u^2$

$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} m(u \cos \beta)^2=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{2} m u^2$

$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \beta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \beta=30^{\circ}$

Hence, $v=u-a t=4-4 t$ (with $t=0$ at 4 s )

$v=0$ at $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$ or at 5 s from origin.

At the end of $6 \mathrm{~s}($ or $t=2 \mathrm{~s}), v=-4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

en.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since, two cylinders are in parallel combination }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{1}{R_e}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{k_e A_e}{l}=\frac{k_1 A_1}{l}+\frac{k_2 A_2}{l} \\ \Rightarrow & k_e \pi(2 R)^2=k_1 \pi(R)^2+k_2 \pi\left[(2 R)^2-(R)^2\right] \\ \Rightarrow & 4 k_e=k_1+3 k_2 \\ \therefore & k_e=\frac{k_1+3 k_2}{4} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

en.content:
A dielectric slab of dielectric constant $k=3$ is filling $\frac{3}{4}$ th space of the capacitor.

When capacitor is charged, then \% of total energy stored in dielectric is
en.explanation:
Energy density in air gap $=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2=u_E$

Energy density of dielectric $=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 K \frac{E^2}{K^2}=\frac{u_E}{K}$

Now, energy stored $=$ energy density × volume

∴ Total energy stored

$=\frac{3}{4} \cdot x \cdot \frac{u_E}{K}+\frac{1}{4} \cdot x \cdot u_E[x=$ volume of capacitor $]$

$=x u_E\left(\frac{3}{4 K}+\frac{1}{4}\right) \therefore K=3$;

$=x u_E\left(\frac{2}{4}\right)=\frac{x u_E}{2}$

Energy stored in dielectric $=\frac{3}{4} \cdot x \cdot \frac{u_E}{K}=\frac{x u_E}{4}$

So, \% of energy stored in the dielectric

$$ =\frac{\frac{x u_E}{4}}{\frac{x u_E}{2}} \times 100=50 \% $$

en.content: The degeneracy of a hydrogen atom whose energy equals $-R_{\mathrm{H}}$ / 16
en.explanation:
The energy of an electron in a hydrogen atom is given by the formula:

$$E_n = -\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{n^2}$$

We are told that the energy of the atom is equal to $$-\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{16}$$. To find the principal quantum number $ n $, we compare the two expressions:

$$-\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{n^2} = -\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{16}$$

Canceling out $ -R_{\mathrm{H}} $ from both sides gives:

$$\frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{1}{16}$$

Therefore, $ n^2 = 16 \Rightarrow n = 4 $.

In a hydrogen atom, all orbitals that share the same principal quantum number $ n $ have the same energy. This means they are called degenerate. The total number of these degenerate orbitals is given by $ n^2 $.

So, for $ n = 4 $:

Degeneracy $ = n^2 = 4^2 = 16 $.

en.content: A 1 molar aqueous solution of magnesium nitrate is $40 \%$ dissociated at 298 K . Calculate the osmotic pressure of the solution. $\left(R=0.0821 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\right)$
en.explanation:
Magnesium nitrate separates into ions in solution as shown below:

$$ \mathrm{Mg}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} + 2\mathrm{NO}_3^{-} $$

This means that one unit of magnesium nitrate produces a total of 3 particles after complete dissociation (1 magnesium ion and 2 nitrate ions).

If the salt is only 40% dissociated, it means $\alpha = 0.4$.

The Van’t Hoff factor, $ i $, which shows how many times the number of effective particles increases due to dissociation, is given by:

$$ i = 1 + \alpha (n - 1) $$

Substituting the values, we get:

$$ i = 1 + 0.4(3 - 1) = 1 + 0.8 = 1.8 $$

Balance the following redox reaction in acidic medium and determine the stoichiometric coefficient of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ in the final balanced equation.

$$ \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q) $$
en.explanation:
First, separate into two half-reactions:

Oxidation $\left(\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right.$ to $\left.\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\right)$,

$$ \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+1 e^{-} $$

Reduction ( $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$to $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ ),

$$ \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+} $$

Balance O by adding $4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$,

$$ \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$

Balance H by adding $8 \mathrm{H}^{+}$,

$$ 8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$

Add $5 e^{-}$to balance charge,

$$ 8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+5 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$

Now, multiply oxidation half-reaction by 5 :

$$ 5 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \longrightarrow 5 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+5 e^{-} $$

Now add both:

$$ 8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+5 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+5 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$

Thus, the stoichiometric coefficient of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=4$

en.content: If the de-Broglie wavelength of a particle is equal to $10 \sqrt{h / m}$, then what is the ratio of its speed to mass?
en.explanation:
The de-Broglie wavelength is given by the formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{mV}$$

According to the question, the wavelength is also given as:

$$\lambda = 10 \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Now, equating the two expressions for $ \lambda $:

$$\frac{h}{mV} = 10 \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Multiply both sides by $ mV $:

$$h = 10 \, mV \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Divide both sides by 10:

$$\frac{h}{10} = mV \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Square both sides to remove the square root:

$$\left(\frac{h}{10}\right)^2 = (mV)^2 \times \frac{h}{m}$$

Simplify the equation:

$$\frac{h^2}{100} = m^2V^2 \frac{h}{m}$$

$$\frac{h}{100} = mV^2$$

Now, solve for $ V $:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{h}{100m}}$$

To find the ratio of speed to mass, $ \frac{V}{m} $:

$$\frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{m} \sqrt{\frac{h}{100m}} = \frac{\sqrt{h}}{10 \, m^{3/2}}$$

en.content: The bond dissociation energies of $A_2, B_2$ and $A B$ are in the ratio $1: 4: 2$. If $\Delta H$ for formation of $A B$ from $A_2$ and $B_2$ is $-100 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, calculate the bond dissociation energy of $B_2$.
en.explanation:
Let, $A_2=x$, then $B_2=4 x, A B=2 x$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H & =\left[\frac{1}{2} A_2+\frac{1}{2} B_2 \longrightarrow A B\right] \\ & =\left(\frac{1}{2} x+2 x\right)-2 x=\frac{1}{2} x=-100 \rightarrow x=-200 \end{aligned} $$

So, $B_2=4 x=-800 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$

en.content: A current of 4.0 A is passed through 0.5 L of 0.2 M NaCl solution for 1200 s . Calculate the pH of the solution after electrolysis.
en.explanation:
Electrolysis of NaCl produces NaOH at the cathode.

Charge passed $=4 \times 1200=4800 \mathrm{C}$

Moles of electrons $=\frac{4800}{96500} \approx 0.04974 \mathrm{~mol}$

Since 2 moles of electron produce 2 moles NaOH , NaOH formed $\boldsymbol{\approx} 0.04974 \mathrm{~mol}$

$$ \begin{gathered} \text { Concentration }=\frac{0.04974}{0.5}=0.0995 \mathrm{M} \\ \text { pOH }=-\log 0.0995 \\ \text { pOH } \approx 1, \text { so } \mathrm{pH} \approx 14-1=13 \end{gathered} $$

en.content: Identify the compound that undergoes self-aldol condensation in the presence of cold dilute alkali, forming a $\beta$-hydroxy aldehyde:
en.explanation:
Aldol condensation requires the presence of at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen in the aldehyde. Among the options, acetaldehyde $\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}\right)$ has $\alpha$-hydrogens and undergoes the reaction.

$$ 2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}+\text { dilute } \mathrm{NaOH}\longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHO} \text { (3-hydroxy butanal) } $$

This $\beta$-hydroxy aldehyde may further dehydrate to form an $\alpha, \beta$-unsaturated aldehyde:

$$ \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCHO}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$

The other compounds either lack $\alpha$-hydrogens or are deactivated (e.g., benzaldehyde, phenylacetylene derivative).

en.content: If for a first-order reaction, the frequency factor $A=4 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and activation energy $E_a=98.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, at what temperature will the half-life be 10 minutes?
en.explanation:
Half-life for first-order reaction,

$$ t_{1 / 2}=0.693 / \mathrm{k} $$

Given, $t_{1 / 2}=600 \mathrm{~s}$

$$ k=\frac{0.693}{600} \approx 1.155 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

Using Arrhenius,

$$ k=A e^{\left(-E_0 / R T\right)} $$

Take $\log$ both sides and solve,

$$ \ln k=\ln (A)-\frac{E_a}{R T} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Substituting the values, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \ln \left(1.155 \times 10^{-3}\right) & =\ln \left(4 \times 10^{13}\right)-\left(\frac{98600}{R T}\right) \\ & \approx 311.49 k \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: If $f: X \rightarrow Y$ be a function defined by $f(x)=a \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+b \cos x+c$ and $f$ is bijective, then the set $X$ with $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right)$ is
en.explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=a \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+b \cos x+c \\ & f(x)=\frac{a \sin x}{\sqrt{2}}+\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}+b\right) \cos x+c=r \sin (x+\theta)+c \\ & \text { where } r=\sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}+b\right)^2} \text { and } \\ & \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

For $f$ to be one-one, we must have

$$ \begin{aligned} & -\frac{\pi}{2} \leq x+\theta \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \Rightarrow x \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta, \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right] \end{aligned} $$

Hence, $X=\left[\frac{-\pi}{2}-\theta, \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right]$, where

$$ \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right) $$

en.content: The function $f(x)=\frac{x}{\sin x}$ is strictly increasing in the interval.
en.explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { We have, } f(x)=\frac{x}{\sin x} \\ & \therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\sin x-x \cos x}{\sin ^2 x}=\frac{g(x)}{\sin ^2 x} \text {, where } g(x) \\ & =\sin x-x \cos x \\ & \text { Now, } g(x)=\sin x-x \cos x \\ & g^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-\cos x+x \sin x=x \sin x \\ & \Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)>0 \text { for all } x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \qquad\left[\because \sin x>0 \text { for } x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow g(x)$ is an increasing function on $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow g(x)>g(0), \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow g(x)>0, \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{g(x)}{\sin ^2 x}>0, \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \quad \quad\left[\because \sin ^2 x>0 \text { for } x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right] \\ & \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0, \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Hence, $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

en.explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { } \int_a^b e^x d x=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h\left[e^a+e^{a+h}+e^{a+2 h}+\ldots+e^{a+(n-1) h}\right] \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h e^a\left[1+e^h+e^{2 h}+e^{3 h}+\ldots+e^{(n-1) h}\right] \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h e^a\left\{\frac{\left(e^h\right)^n-1}{e^h-1}\right\} \\ & \quad\left[\because a+a r+\ldots+a r^{n-1}=a\left(\frac{r^n-1}{r-1}\right)\right] \end{aligned} $$

en.content: For what values of the parameter ' $a$ ' does the function $f(x)=x^3+3(a-7) x^2+3\left(a^2-9\right) x-1$ have a positive point of maximum.
en.explanation:
We have,

$$ \begin{aligned} f(x) & =x^3+3(a-7) x^2+3\left(a^2-9\right) x-1 \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =3 x^2+6(a-7) x+3\left(a^2-9\right) \\ & =3\left[x^2+2(a-7) x+\left(a^2-9\right)\right] \end{aligned} $$

For local maximum, we must have $f^{\prime}(x)=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x^2+2(a-7) x+a^2-9=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-(a-7) \pm \sqrt{58-14 a} \end{aligned} $$

Let $x_1=-(a-7)+\sqrt{58-14 a}$ and

$$ x_2=-(a-7)-\sqrt{58-14 a} $$

For $x_1, x_2$ to be real, we must have

$$ 58-14 a>0 \Rightarrow a<\frac{58}{14}=\frac{29}{7} $$

Now, $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=3[2 x+2(a-7)]=6[x+a-7]$

Clearly, $f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_2\right)=6\left(x_2+a-7\right)=-6 \sqrt{58-14 a}<0$

So, $x_2=-(a-7)-\sqrt{58-14 a}$ is a point of local maximum.

Now, $x_2>0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-(a-7)-\sqrt{58-14 a}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 7-a>\sqrt{58-14 a} \Rightarrow(7-a)^2>58-14 a \\ & \Rightarrow a^2-9>0 \Rightarrow a<-3 \text { or } a>3 \\ & \text { Hence, } a \in(-\infty,-3) \cup\left(3, \frac{29}{7}\right) \end{aligned} $$

en.content: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+x \sin 2 y=x^3 \cos ^2 y$ is
en.explanation:
We have $\frac{d y}{d x}+x \sin 2 y=x^3 \cos ^2 y$

Dividing both sides by $\cos ^2 y$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}+x \frac{\sin 2 y}{\cos ^2 y}=x^3 \\ & \Rightarrow \sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x \tan y=x^3 \end{aligned} $$

Put $\tan y=v$

and $\sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}$, we get

$$ \frac{d v}{d x}+(2 x) v=x^3 $$

Now, $\mathrm{IF}=e^{\int 2 x d x}=e^{x^2}$

Multiplying both sides by IF, we get

$$ e^{x^2} \frac{d v}{d x}+2 x v \cdot e^{x^2}=e^{x^2} \cdot x^3 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Integrating both sides w.r.t } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & v e^{x^2}=\int x^3 e^{x^2} d x+C \\ &=\int x^3 e^t\left(\frac{d t}{2 x}\right)+C, \text { where } t=x^2 \\ &=\frac{1}{2} \int t e^t d t+C=\frac{1}{2}\left(t e^t-e^t\right)+C \\ &=\frac{1}{2}(t-1) e^t+C=\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2-1\right) e^{x^2}+C \\ & \Rightarrow \quad e^{x^2} \tan y=\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2-1\right) e^{x^2}+C \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: If ' $a$ ' is a complex number such that $|a|=1$. Find the value of $a$, so that the equation $a z^2+z+1=0$ has one purely imaginary root.
en.explanation:
Let $\alpha$ be a purely imaginary root of $a z^2+z+1=0$

Then,   $$ a \alpha^2+\alpha+1=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Taking conjugate on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \bar{a}(\bar{\alpha})^2+\bar{\alpha}+1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \bar{a} \alpha^2-\alpha+1=0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[\because \bar{\alpha}=-\alpha]\end{array} $$

Solving, Eqs. (i) and (ii) by cross-multiplication, we get

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} \frac{\alpha^2}{1+1} & =\frac{\alpha}{\bar{a}-a}=\frac{1}{-a-\bar{a}} \\ \Rightarrow & \alpha^2 =\frac{-2}{a+\bar{a}} \text { and } \alpha=\frac{\bar{a}-a}{-a-\bar{a}} \end{array} $$

Now, $\alpha^2=(\alpha)^2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{-2}{a+\bar{a}}=\left(\frac{\bar{a}-a}{-a-\bar{a}}\right)^2=\frac{(\bar{a}-a)^2}{(a+\bar{a})^2} \\ & \Rightarrow-2(a+\bar{a})=(\bar{a}-a)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(\bar{a}-a)^2+2(\bar{a}+a)=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

Now, $|a|=1 \Rightarrow a=\cos \theta+i \sin \theta$

Put the value of $a$ in Eq. (iii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & (-2 i \sin \theta)^2+4 \cos \theta=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -4 \sin ^2 \theta+4 \cos \theta=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos ^2 \theta+\cos \theta-1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}, \frac{-\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \quad[\because \cos \theta \in[-1,1]] \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Hence, $a=\cos \left\{\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right)\right\}$$$ +i \sin \left\{\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right)\right\} $$

en.content: If $\mathbf{a , b , c}$ are vectors such that $|\mathbf{b}|=|\mathbf{c}|$ then $\{(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) \times(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{c})\} \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})$ is equal to
en.explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \begin{array}{l} \{(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) \times(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{c})\} \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}) \\ \\ =(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{c}+\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}) \\ \begin{array}{r} \{(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{c}) \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})+(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{a}) \times(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})+(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) \\ \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})\} \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}) \end{array} \\ =\{[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{c} \mathbf{c}] \mathbf{b}-[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{c} \mathbf{b}] \mathbf{c}+[\mathbf{b} \mathbf{a} \mathbf{c}] \mathbf{b}-[\mathbf{b} \mathbf{a} \mathbf{b}] \mathbf{c}\} \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}) \end{array} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\{-[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{c} \mathbf{b}] \mathbf{c}+[\mathbf{b} \mathbf{a} \mathbf{c}] \mathbf{b}\} \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}) \\ & =-[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{c} \mathbf{b}]\{\mathbf{c} \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})\}+[\mathbf{b} \mathbf{a} \mathbf{c}]\{\mathbf{b} \cdot(\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})\} \\ & =[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]\left(\mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{b}+|\mathbf{c}|^2\right)-[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]\left(|\mathbf{b}|^2+\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{c}\right) \\ & =[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]\left(|\mathbf{c}|^2-|\mathbf{b}|^2\right) \\ & =[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}] \times 0=0 \quad[\because|\mathbf{b}|=|\mathbf{c}|] \end{aligned} $$

en.content: For what value of $a, 6$ lies between the roots of the equation $x^2+2(a-3) x+9=0$.
en.explanation:
Let $f(x)=x^2+2(a-3) x+9$.

If 6 lies between the roots of $f(x)=0$, then we must have the following conditions:

(i) Disc $>0$ and (ii) $f(6)<0$

[ $\because$ coefficient of $x^2$ is positive]

Now (i) Disc $>0$

$$ \begin{array}{lc} \Rightarrow & 4(a-3)^2-36>0 \\ \Rightarrow & (a-3)^2-9>0 \\ \Rightarrow & a^2-6 a>0 \\ \Rightarrow & a<0 \text { or } a>6\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \\ \text { and Eq. (ii) } & f(6)<0 \\ \Rightarrow & 36+12(a-3)+9<0 \\ \Rightarrow & 12 a+9<0 \\ \Rightarrow & a<-\frac{3}{4} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{array} $$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

                               $$ a<-\frac{3}{4} $$

i.e.                                $$ a \in\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{4}\right) $$

en.content: What is the probability of getting a sum of 9 in a single throw of three fair dice?
en.explanation:
To get a sum of 9 ,possible dice combinations are

$$ \begin{array}{ll} (1,3,6) & -6 \text { ways } \\ (1,3,5) & -5 \text { ways } \\ (1,4,4) & -3 \text { ways } \\ (2,3,5) & -3 \text { ways } \\ (2,3,4) & -6 \text { ways } \\ (3,3,3) & -1 \text { way } \end{array} $$

Total ways $=6+6+3+3+6+1=25$

Total possible outcomes when rolling three dice $=6 \times 6 \times 6=216$.

Number of favourable outcomes $=25$

∴ Probability of getting a sum of 9 in a single throw of three fair dice $=\frac{25}{216}$

en.content: The coefficient of $x^n$ in the expansion of $\frac{1-a x-x^2}{e^x}$ is
en.explanation:
Taylor series expansion of $e^{-x}$ is

$$ e^{-x}=1-\frac{x}{1!}+\frac{x^2}{2!}-\frac{x^3}{3!}+\ldots=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^k x^k}{k!} $$

Now, $\frac{1-a x-x^2}{e^x}=\left(1-a x-x^2\right) e^{-x}$

                           $$ =e^{-x}-(a x) e^{-x}-\left(x^2\right) e^{-x} $$

From $e^{-x}$, the coefficient of $x^n$ in $e^{-x}$ is $\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}$

From $-a x e^{-x}$, the coefficient of $x^n$ in $-a x e^{-x}$ is given by the coefficient of $x^{n-1}$ in $e^{-x}$, multiplied by $-a$

$$ -a \cdot \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} $$

From $-x^2 e^{-x}$, the coefficient of $x^n$ in $-x^2 e^{-x}$ is given by the coefficient of $x^{n-2}$ in $e^{-x}$, multiplied by -1 :

$$ \frac{-(-1)^{n-2}}{(n-2)!} $$

Now, combine the coefficient from all three terms

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { coefficient of } x^n=\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}-a \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}-\frac{(-1)^{n-2}}{(n-2)} \\ & =(-1)^{n-2}\left[\frac{(-1)^2}{n!}-a \cdot \frac{(-1)}{(n-1)!}-1 \cdot \frac{1}{(n-2)!}\right] \\ & =(-1)^{n-2}\left[\frac{1}{n!}+\frac{a}{(n-1)!}-\frac{1}{(n-2)!}\right] \\ & =\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}\left[1+(a+1) n-n^2\right] \end{aligned} $$

en.content: The locus of the middle points of chords of the circle $x^2+y^2=25$ which are parallel to the line $x-2 y+3=0$ is
en.explanation:
Let $(h, k)$ be the mid-point of a chord of the circle $x^2+y^2=25$.

Then, equation of the chord is $h x+k y-25$

$$ =h^2+k^2-25 \Rightarrow h x+k y=h^2+k^2 $$

If this is parallel to $x-2 y+3=0$, then $-\frac{h}{k}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 2 h+k=0$

So, the locus of $(h, k)$ is $2 x+y=0$.

en.content: What is the value of the definite integral $\int_0^\pi \log (\sin x) d x$ ?
en.explanation:
We have, $I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\sin x) d x$

Using the property:

$$ \int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x $$

we replace $x$ with $\frac{\pi}{2}-x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\cos x) d x \\ \Rightarrow & I+I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}[\log (\cos x)+\log (\sin x)] d x \\ = & \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x\right) d x=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) d x+\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\sin 2 x) d x \\ = & -\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2+\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\sin x) d x \\ \Rightarrow & 2 I=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2+I \\ \Rightarrow & I=-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2 \end{aligned} $$

en.content: The number of real solution of $\sqrt{\left(7-\log _3|x|\right)}=4-\log _3|x|$ is equal to
en.explanation:
Since, $7-\log _3|x| \geq 0 \Rightarrow \log _3|x| \leq 7$

And $4-\log _3|x| \geq 0 \Rightarrow \log _3|x| \leq 4$

$$ \Rightarrow|x| \leq 3^4=81 $$

Now, $7-\log _3|x|=\left(4-\log _3|x|\right)^2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 7-\log _3|x|=16-8 \log _3|x|+\left(\log _3|x|\right)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\log _3|x|\right)^2-7 \log _3|x|+9=0 \end{aligned} $$

Let $t=\log _3|x|$, then

$$ \begin{aligned} & t^2-7 t+9=0 \\ & \Rightarrow t=\frac{7 \pm \sqrt{49-36}}{2}=\frac{7 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Since, $t=\log _3|x|$ must be a real number and we know that $0 \leq \log _3|x| \leq 4$

So, only roots satisfy the condition is

$$ 0 \leq \frac{7-\sqrt{13}}{2} \leq 4 $$

and this root provides two solutions for $x$.

$[\because|x|$ means both $x$ and $-x$ satisfying the equation $]$

Thus, the number of real solutions is 2 .

en.content: What is the coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $(1+x)^{41}\left(1-x+x^2\right)^{40}$.
en.explanation:
We have, $(1+x)^{41}\left(1-x+x^2\right)^{40}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\left\{(1+x)\left(1-x+x^2\right)\right\}^{40}(1+x) \\ & =\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}(1+x) \\ & =\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}+x\left(1+x^3\right)^{40} \end{aligned} $$

∴ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $(1+x)^{41}\left(1-x+x^2\right)^{40}$

= Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}+$ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $x\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}$

$=$ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}+$ Coefficient of $x^{49}$ in $\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}$

= Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\sum_{r=0}^{40}{ }^{40} C_r x^{3 r}+$ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\sum_{r=0}^{40}{ }^{40} C_r x^{3 r}=0+0=0$

en.content: If the equation $2 h x y+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$ represents two straight lines, then the area of rectangle formed with the coordinates axes is
en.explanation:
The equation $2 h x y+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$ will represents a pair of straight lines if

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0+2 f g h-0 \times f^2-0 \times g^2-c h^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 f g=c h \\ & \Rightarrow \quad c=\frac{2 f g}{h} \\ & \quad 2 h x y+2 g x+2 f y+\frac{2 f g}{h}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow h^2 x y+g h x+f h y+f g=0 \\ & \Rightarrow h y(h x+f)+g(h x+f)=0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow(h x+f)(h y+g)=0 $$

Thus, the equations of lines represented by the given equation are $h x+f=0$ and $h y+g=0$.

These two lines and the coordinate axes form a rectangle $O A B C$.

∴ Area of rectangle $O A B C=O A \times O C$

$$ =\left|\frac{-f}{h}\right| \times\left|\frac{-g}{h}\right|=\frac{|f g|}{h^2} $$

en.explanation:
The point $(2 a, a+1)$ will be an interior point of the larger segment of the circle.

$$ x^2+y^2-2 x-2 y-8=0 $$

if the point $(2 a, a+1)$ is an interior point and the point $(2 a, a+1)$ and the centre $(1,1)$ are on the same side of the chord $x-y+1=0$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore(2 a)^2+(a+1)^2-2(2 a)-2(a+1)-8 < 0 \\ & \text { and }(2 a-a-1+1)(1-1+1) > 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 a^2-4 a-9 < 0 \text { and } a > 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(5 a-9)(a+1)<0 \text { and } a > 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad-1 < a <\frac{9}{5} \text { and } a > 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a \in\left(0, \frac{9}{5}\right) \end{aligned} $$

en.content: Tangents are drawn to the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ at points where it is intersected by the line $l x+m y+n=0$. The point of intersection of tangents at these points is
en.explanation:
Let $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ be the point of intersection of tangents to the ellipse, where it is intersected by the line $l x+m y+n=0$. Then, the equation of the chord of contact of tangents is

$$ \frac{x x_1}{a^2}+\frac{y y_1}{b^2}=1\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) $$

Clearly, $l x+m y+n=0$ and (i) represents the same line.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{x_1}{a^2 l}=\frac{y_1}{b^2 m}=\frac{1}{-n} \\ \Rightarrow & x_1=\frac{-a^2 l}{n}, y_1=\frac{-b^2 m}{n} \end{array} $$

Thus, the required point is $\left(\frac{-a^2 l}{n}, \frac{-b^2 m}{n}\right)$

en.content: The locus of the mid-point of the chords of the circle $x^2+y^2=16$ which are tangents to the hyperbola $9 x^2-16 y^2=144$ is $\left(x^2+y^2\right)^2=k x^2-l y^2$. Then, the sum of values of $k$ and $l$ is
en.explanation:
Let $(h, k)$ be the mid-point of a chord of the circle $x^2+y^2=16$.

Then, the equation of the chord is

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} & h x+k y-16 =h^2+k^2-16 \\& h x+k y =h^2+k^2 \\ \Rightarrow & y =\left(\frac{-h}{k}\right)+\left(\frac{h^2+k^2}{k}\right) \end{array} $$

This will be a tangent to the hyperbola $\frac{x^2}{4^2}-\frac{y^2}{3^2}=1$, if

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{h^2+k^2}{k}\right)^2=4^2 \times\left(\frac{-h}{k}\right)^2-3^2\left[\text { using } c^2=a^2 m^2-b^2\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left(h^2+k^2\right)^2=16 h^2-9 k^2 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the locus of $(h, k)$ is

$$ \left(x^2+y^2\right)^2=16 x^2-9 y^2=k x^2-l y^2 $$

Thus, $k=16, l=9$ and $k+l=16+9=25$

en.content: The angles of a triangle are in the ratio $1: 2: 7$. The ratio of the greatest side to the least side is $(k+1):(k-1)$. The value of $k$ is
en.explanation:
Let the angles of a triangle be $x, 2 x$ and $7 x$.

Then, $x+2 x+7 x=180^{\circ} \Rightarrow x=18^{\circ}$

So, angles are $A=18^{\circ}, B=36^{\circ}$ and $C=126^{\circ}$

The least side is $a$ and the greatest side is $c$.

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl}&\frac{a}{\sin A} =\frac{c}{\sin C} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{c}{a} =\frac{\sin C}{\sin A}=\frac{\sin 126^{\circ}}{\sin 18^{\circ}} \\ & =\frac{\sin \left(180^{\circ}-54^{\circ}\right)}{\sin 18^{\circ}}=\frac{\sin 54^{\circ}}{\sin 18^{\circ}} \\ & =\frac{\cos 36^{\circ}}{\sin 18^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{(\sqrt{5}+1)}{4}}{\frac{(\sqrt{5}-1)}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{\sqrt{5}-1}=\frac{k+1}{k-1} \\ \therefore \quad k & =\sqrt{5} \end{array} $$

en.content:
Consider the function $g(x)$ defined as

$$ g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x^2-4}{x^2-2|x-2|-4}, & x \neq 2 \\ \frac{3}{4}, & x=2 \end{array}\right. $$

Which of the following statements is true about the continuity of $g(x)$ ?

options.content: $g(x)$ is continuous for all values of $x$.

en.explanation:
When $x<2|x-2|=-(x-2)=2-x$.

Then for $x<2$ :

$$ \begin{aligned} g(x) & =\frac{x^2-4}{x^2-2(2-x)-4}=\frac{x^2-4}{x^2+2 x-8} \\ & =\frac{(x-2)(x+2)}{(x-2)(x+4)}=\frac{x+2}{x+4} \end{aligned} $$

The limit from the left is

$$ \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} g(x)=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \frac{x+2}{x+4}=\frac{2+2}{2+4}=\frac{4}{6}=\frac{2}{3} $$

When $x>2|x-2|=x-2$.

Then, for $x>2$ :

$$ \begin{aligned} g(x) & =\frac{x^2-4}{x^2-2(x-2)-4}=\frac{x^2-4}{x^2-2 x} \\ & =\frac{(x-2)(x+2)}{x(x-2)}=\frac{x+2}{x} \end{aligned} $$

The limit from the right is:

$$ \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} g(x)=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{x+2}{x}=\frac{2+2}{2}=\frac{4}{2}=2 $$

Since, $\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} g(x)=\frac{2}{3}$ and $\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} g(x)=2$, the limit $\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2}} g(x)$ does not exist because the left and right limits are not equal.

Since, the limit does not exists at $x=2, g(x)$ is not continuous at $x=2$

$$ \mathbf{B}=(0-(-2), 6-3,-3-3)=(2,3,-6) $$

Now, $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}=(-2)(2)+(2)(3)+(-1)(-6)$

$$ =-4+6+6=8 $$

And, $|\mathbf{B}|=\sqrt{(2)^2+(3)^2+(-6)^2}$

$$ =\sqrt{4+9+36}=\sqrt{49}=7 $$

The magnitude of projection of vector $\mathbf{A}$ onto $\mathbf{B}$ is given by

$$ \left|\frac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{|\mathbf{B}|}\right| $$

$$ \text { Thus, the magnitude of projection }=\left|\frac{8}{7}\right|=\frac{8}{7} $$

The Boolean expression

$$ \sim(p \wedge q) \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q) $$

is equivalent to
en.explanation:
Using DeMorgan's law,

$$ \sim(p \wedge q)=\sim p \vee \sim q $$

Thus, our expression becomes

$$ (\sim p \vee \sim q) \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q) $$

Rearrange and group similar terms

$$ \sim p \vee(\sim q \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q)) $$

Since, $\sim q \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q)$ simplifies to $\sim q \vee p$, we get

$$ \sim p \vee q $$

The Boolean expression simplifies to $\sim p \vee q$.

en.content: In a binomial distribution, the mean is 10 and the variance is 6 . Then, its median is
en.explanation:
We know that

Mean, $\mu=n p=10\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

Variance, $\sigma^2=n p(1-p)=6\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$

Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i),

$$ \begin{gathered} (1-p)=\frac{6}{10}=0.6 \\ p=0.4 \end{gathered} $$

Substituting $p=0.4 \mathrm{in}$ Eq. (i),

$$ \begin{aligned} n \times 0.4 & =10 \\ n & =25 \end{aligned} $$

The median of a binomial distribution is typically approximated by

$$ M \approx[n p] $$

Substituting values

$$ M=[25 \times 0.4]=[10] $$

Thus, the median is 10 .

en.content:
A rectangle is inscribed in an ellipse with the equation $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$

What is the maximum area of the rectangle that can be inscribed in the ellipse?
en.explanation:
Let the rectangle have vertices at $( \pm x, \pm y)$ on the ellipse. From the ellipse equation

$$ x=a \cos \theta, y=b \sin \theta $$

The area of the rectangle is

$$ A=4 x y=4(a \cos \theta \cdot b \sin \theta) $$

Using the identity $\sin 2 \theta=2 \sin \theta \cos \theta$, we rewrite $A=2 a b \sin 2 \theta$

To maximize $A$, we maximize $\sin 2 \theta$, which reaches its maximum value of 1 when $2 \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$.

Thus, the maximum area is

$$ A_{\max }=2 a b $$

en.content: A four digit number is formed with digits $1,3,4$, 5 with no repetition. What is the probability that the number is divisible by 5 ?
en.explanation:
Total number of ways in which four digit number can be found $=4!=24$

So, exhaustive number of cases $=24$

Now, the number is divisible by 5 if at units place we have 5.

∴ Unit place can be filled in one way the remaining 3 places can be filled with other digits in 3! ways.

So, the number of ways of forming the number divisible by 5 is $3!=6$

So, the required probability $=\frac{6}{24}=\frac{1}{4}$

en.content: The slope of the curve $2 y^2=a x^2+b$ at $(1,-1)$ is -1 . Then, the value of $b$ is
en.explanation:

Differentiate w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4 y \frac{d y}{d x}=2 a x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x}{2 y} \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(1,-1)}=\frac{-a}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Also, since the slope of the tangent at $(1,-1)$ is -1 .

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{-a}{2}=-1 \Rightarrow a=2 $$

Since, the point $(1,-1)$ lies on Eq. (i)

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2(-1)^2=a(1)^2+b=a+b=2 \\ \Rightarrow & 2+b=2 \Rightarrow b=0 \end{array} $$

en.content: If four lines $a x \pm b y \pm c=0$ encloses a rhombus, then the area is
en.explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { The equations of the four lines are }\\ &\begin{aligned} & a x+b y+c=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & a x-b y+c=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & a x+b y-c=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\\ & a x-b y-c=0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iv) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Clearly, lines (i) and (iii) are parallel. Also, lines (ii) and (iv) are also parallel.

So, the given lines form a parallelogram whose vertices are $A\left(\frac{c}{a}, 0\right), B\left(0, \frac{c}{b}\right), C\left(\frac{-c}{a}, 0\right)$ and $D\left(0, \frac{-c}{b}\right)$.

Clearly, diagonal $A C$ and $B D$ of this 11 gm are at right angle to each other. So, the given lines form a rhombus $A B C D$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Area of rhombus } A B C D & =\frac{1}{2}(A C \times B D) \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2 c}{a} \times \frac{2 c}{b}\right)=\frac{2 c^2}{a b} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

Similarly, $\mathrm{FROCK}=45$

Number of letters $=5$

$$ 5 \times 9=45 $$
